title: Problems with Memory Quota
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

I tried to run a container with the memory quota (--memory) and then tried to run some corresponding tests. But it seems that it is possible to allocate more memory than is was set with the mentioned parameter. Swap is disabled in OS.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Build a simple container: Containerfile has the only command: `FROM ubuntu:latest`

2. Disable swap: `sudo swapoff -a` (host OS is Ubuntu too)

3. Start this container with the --memory option:
`podman run -dt --memory=10m -v $HOME:$HOME localhost/test:latest`
`podman attach 12345678`

5. Try to allocate RAM by the following commands:

either
> sh -c "MEMBLOB=\$(dd if=/dev/urandom bs=100MB count=1)"

or

> date ; </dev/zero head -c 100m | tail ; date

6. Try to allocate RAM by the simple C++ program which just uses the `new` operator and catches an exception if any occurs.

**Describe the results you received:**

It is possible to allocate much more memory than it was set with `--memory` before the tests begin to fail.

**Describe the results you expected:**

(Citation from the official documentation: [https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-run.1.html) )

> --memory, -m=number[unit]
> ( . . . )
> Allows you to constrain the memory available to a container

Since the `--memory` option is set and swap is disabled I expect that it should be impossible to allocate more than the constrained amount of RAM.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.4.4
API Version:  3.4.4
Go Version:   go1.17.3
Built:        Thu Jan  1 03:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info`:**

```
podman/jammy,now 3.4.4+ds1-1ubuntu1 amd64 [installed]
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**


Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Using VirtualBox. Host OS for the VirtualBox is Windows 10.
OS inside VirtualBox is Ubuntu 22.04, container has been built with the same one (`ubuntu:latest` refers to 22.04 at the moment)

Also this behavior is reproduced on the ARM-based board working on Linux kernel

comments.body: could you show the output of `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max` inside the container?

```
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman run -dt --memory=10m localhost/test:latest
66abe8514e450de132e917b2c307d33a63daf5b17898a0ab10f7dbee3b1ea701
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman attach 66
root@66abe8514e45:/# cat /sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max 
10485760
root@66abe8514e45:/#
```

the cgroup configuration seems fine.

With a slight modification to your reproducer, could you try `date ; </dev/zero head -c 100m | tail && echo ok ; date` ?

Could you also trying adding `--cgroup-conf memory.oom.group=1` to the run command so the entire container is destroyed on OOM: `podman run ... --cgroup-conf memory.oom.group=1 --memory=10m`

Another thing you'd like to check is the value of `/proc/sys/vm/oom_kill_allocating_task`

```
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ cat /proc/sys/vm/oom_kill_allocating_task 
0
```
Starting the container:
```
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman run -dt --memory=10m --cgroup-conf memory.oom.group=1 -v $HOME:$HOME localhost/test:latest
```
Trying the test command:
```
root@a4f1d7902950:~# </dev/zero head -c 1m | tail && echo ok

artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                   COMMAND     CREATED        STATUS            PORTS       NAMES
a4f1d7902950  localhost/test:latest  bash        3 minutes ago  Up 3 minutes ago              vibrant_moser
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$
```
If I try to allocate up to 9 MB it causes logout without stopping the container. But when I try to allocate 9 MB or more it causes stopping the container with error code 137.
But this command behaves strange itself. For example, if I run another similar container with 100MB memory quota and try to allocate just 1 MB, this command could cause logout from the container without its stopping:
```
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman run -dt --memory=100m --cgroup-conf memory.oom.group=1 -v $HOME:$HOME localhost/ololo:latest
d9e6d4e6525a09e9c5676f091272e7c25772910b54ac2aac47b448a3e118bf82
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman attach d9
root@d9e6d4e6525a:/# </dev/zero head -c 1m | tail && echo ok

artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$ podman ps -a
CONTAINER ID  IMAGE                   COMMAND     CREATED         STATUS                           PORTS       NAMES
a4f1d7902950  localhost/test:latest  bash        29 minutes ago  Exited (137) About a minute ago              vibrant_moser
d9e6d4e6525a  localhost/test:latest  bash        54 seconds ago  Up 54 seconds ago                            silly_meitner
artem@artem-VirtualBox:~$
```
The next command also has an unexpected behaviour.
```
sh -c "MEMBLOB=$(dd if=/dev/urandom bs=10MB count=10)"
```
Trying to allocate 10 of 10MB blocks inside the 100MB-quoted container causes its stopping with exit code 129 **_and logout from the host OS user session._**

And the last test with the following C++ program (which tries to allocate certain amount of RAM and catches an exception):
```
#include <iostream>
#include <exception>
#include <stdlib.h>  // atoi()

int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    if (argc < 2) {
        std::cout << "Please specify the 1st argument: size-to-allocate (in MB)\n";
        return 0;
    }
    unsigned int size = atoi(argv[1]);
    std::cout << "Trying to allocate " << size << " MB...\n";
    size *= 1024 * 1024;
    char* blob;
    try {
        blob = new char[size];
    }
    catch (std::exception& e) {
        std::cerr << "Exception caught!\n'" << e.what() << "'\n";
        return 135;
    }
    delete [] blob;
    std::cout << "Memory has been freed\n";
    return 0;
}
```
I tried this program inside the 10MB-quoted container.
It seems that it is able to allocate much more than 10MB, even up to all of free memory (virtual machine has 1 GB RAM).
```
root@a4f1d7902950:/home/artem/test-podman/scripts# ./allocate 10
Trying to allocate 10 MB...
Success!
Memory has been freed
root@a4f1d7902950:/home/artem/test-podman/scripts# ./allocate 100
Trying to allocate 100 MB...
Success!
Memory has been freed
root@a4f1d7902950:/home/artem/test-podman/scripts# ./allocate 1000
Trying to allocate 1000 MB...
Exception caught!
'std::bad_alloc'
root@a4f1d7902950:/home/artem/test-podman/scripts#
```
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
